zh:
阿拉伯传说的底层是前伊斯兰时代半岛上的荒漠信仰：看不见的镇尼与人共享世界，占据废墟、枯井与旷野，诗人从它们那里领受诗句，旅人在夜路上提防变形食人的古尔。伊斯兰教兴起后，这个隐形种族被写进了经典——《古兰经》说镇尼由无烟之火造成，与天使、人类并列为理性的造物，有专章记其皈依；先知王所罗门役使群灵建殿采珠，把叛逆者封进黄铜瓶沉入海底，为后来的故事备好了取之不尽的道具。

这些散落的信仰最终汇入《一千零一夜》。它的框架来自波斯故事集《千个故事》——十世纪的马苏第与伊本·纳迪姆都提到过这部书——再经巴格达与开罗数百年的层层增补，把宰相之女山鲁佐德每夜悬停在破晓处的讲述，变成一座装得下渔夫、脚夫、商人与精灵的故事迷宫。1704年起，安托万·加朗的法译本让它风靡欧洲；《阿拉丁》《阿里巴巴》这批家喻户晓的“孤儿故事”甚至没有阿拉伯底本，是叙利亚人汉纳·迪亚卜在巴黎口述给他的。

这一体系带着商旅的气质：领衔的英雄是巴格达商人辛巴达，连奇迹也标着价钱——飞毯在集市上卖四十袋金币，钻石谷的钻石靠商人抛下肉块粘取；它的宇宙又极辽阔，环抱人世的翡翠卡夫山之外是镇尼的国土，大地层层驮在天使、巨牛与巨鱼巴哈姆特的背上。
en:
At the root of Arabian lore lies the desert belief of the pre-Islamic peninsula: the invisible jinn share the world with humankind, holding the ruins, dry wells and wastelands, dictating verses to poets, while travellers on night roads watch for the shapeshifting, man-eating ghul. With the rise of Islam this hidden race was written into scripture — the Quran declares the jinn created from smokeless fire, rational beings alongside angels and humans, and devotes a chapter to their conversion. The prophet-king Solomon pressed them into service, building sanctuaries and diving for pearls, and sealed the rebels into brass bottles sunk in the sea, stocking the later stories with inexhaustible props.

These scattered beliefs pooled at last in the Thousand and One Nights. Its frame descends from the Persian Hazar Afsan, 'A Thousand Tales', noted by al-Mas'udi and Ibn al-Nadim in the tenth century, then thickened over centuries of retelling in Baghdad and Cairo, until Scheherazade's nightly stories, each suspended at daybreak, grew into a labyrinth wide enough for fishermen, porters, merchants and jinn. From 1704 Antoine Galland's French translation swept Europe; the most famous pieces of all — Aladdin, Ali Baba — are 'orphan tales' with no Arabic source text, told to Galland in Paris by the Syrian traveller Hanna Diyab.

The temper of the tradition is mercantile. Its leading hero is Sinbad, a merchant of Baghdad, and even its wonders carry price tags — a flying carpet bought for forty purses of gold, diamonds fished from their valley with slabs of thrown meat. Yet its cosmos is vast: beyond the emerald ring of Mount Qaf lie the kingdoms of the jinn, and the earth rides on the backs of an angel, a bull and the cosmic fish Bahamut.
